turns.content: I'm following along with a talk on Rust error handling and I need help mapping out the logic for collecting errors from multiple services. How do I combine a Vec<Result<SomeType>> into a single Result<Vec<SomeType>> so that if any service fails, the entire operation fails?

You should use the try operator or the iterator collection method to map over your vector and collect results. You can call vec::try_iter() on a Vec<Result<T>> which returns an Iterator<Item=Result<T>> that you can then collect into a Result<Vec<T>> using the collect() pattern, but this only works if all items succeed; otherwise it propagates the first error encountered.

That sounds good for fail-fast behavior. But what if I want to know which service actually failed instead of just stopping at the first one? Can I still use try_iter() or do I need a different approach like using Option::collect()? I'm trying to decide between returning an empty vector and storing partial successes.

You cannot use try_iter() if you want to preserve all errors because it aborts immediately on the first failure. Instead, you should collect into a Vec<Result<T>> and then manually process them using match or if-let chains to separate successful results from failed ones. If you need a Result<Vec<T>>, you can implement your own custom error type that includes both the list of successes and the list of failures.

That makes sense, but I'm worried about complexity. Is there a way to avoid writing this logic manually? Can I leverage something like serde_json::Value or std::error::Error traits to automatically handle the grouping without implementing my own custom types?

There is no built-in trait that automatically converts a Vec<Result<T>> into Result<Vec<T>> while preserving all errors, so you will need to write some logic manually. The std::error::Error trait defines how to format error messages but does not provide methods for collecting multiple errors into a single Result. You must implement your own logic using the From trait or manual iteration if you want to reuse existing error types from crates like thiserror.

Okay, so I'll need to manually iterate and collect successes into one vector and failures into another. But what about fail-last vs fail-first? If I use try_iter(), it's inherently fail-first. How do I implement a scenario where only the last failure matters?

You can achieve fail-last by collecting all errors into a vector first, then checking if any exist before returning an error containing that list, or you could modify your collection logic to append each new error only if the previous one did not succeed. For fail-first behavior with try_iter(), it's straightforward but for fail-last you need to handle the accumulation explicitly in your code.

Thanks, that clarifies things. One last thing: if I'm building a CLI tool using clap, how does this pattern interact with clap::Error? Should I use the Error type or just plain Result for my internal logic before returning to the CLI?

You should keep your internal logic as Result<Vec<T>> and convert it to a custom error structure that implements std::error::Error and Display traits, then construct a clap::Error from that. This allows you to maintain fine-grained control over which errors are captured and how they are reported without letting clap interfere with your collection logic.
I'm struggling with how to handle errors in my async data fetching pipeline. I want to collect a vector of results where each one might fail, but I need the entire batch to succeed or fail together.

You are likely thinking about using Option::collect() on your Vec<Option<T>> which is great for success-only cases, but that won't help you propagate individual errors. For a collection of results where any failure stops the whole process, you should use Result::from_iter() or iterate with collect::<Result<Vec<_>, _>>(). The key distinction is whether you want fail-first (stop at first error) versus fail-last (aggregate all errors into one).
I see. So if I have a Vec<Result<T, E>>, can I just call collect() and get a Result<Vec<T>, E>?

Almost, but you need to be careful with the error type. If you use collect::<Result<Vec<_>, _>>(), it will only propagate the first error encountered into the final Result. That is fail-first behavior which might not match your needs if you want to know exactly which items failed. To combine errors from multiple sources or get a single aggregated error, you would need to chain operations with and_then() or use fold() to collect all E values.
Okay, so I can't just use collect() if I want to see the specific failures. What's the best pattern for a batch of async fetches?

For async batch operations where you want fail-first behavior but still track what went wrong, you should iterate through your collection manually with futures::stream or manual loops. If you use the standard library's collect() on a stream of Results, it stops at the first failure. To get all errors while stopping execution, you can map each result to Result<T, E> and then use fold() to accumulate errors if needed, or simply return early once an error is found in your loop logic.
Wait, I thought collect() would give me a vector of all results even if some failed?

That's the misconception. Result::collect::<Result<Vec<_>, _>>() is designed to return either success with all values or failure with just the first error E. It does not preserve intermediate failures in the output vector. If you need to keep failed items alongside successful ones, you cannot use Result<Vec<T>>; instead you should collect into Vec<Result<T, E>> and handle that pattern yourself.
So I have to manually check each item and stop? That seems inefficient for large batches.

You are correct that a full manual loop is expensive if you only care about stopping early. However, there is a more efficient approach using the std::iter module with fold(). You can iterate over your stream and use fold() to collect errors up until failure while maintaining performance characteristics similar to a simple for loop.
Can you show me how to implement this fail-first logic without collecting every error?

You can use the iterator's collect method with a specific closure or simply use the standard library's from_iter() pattern but wrap it in conditional logic. The most idiomatic way is to map your async items into Results, then call collect::<Result<Vec<_>, _>>() which gives you fail-first behavior automatically without manual error accumulation.
I'm still confused about the difference between Vec<Result<T>> and Result<Vec<T>>. Which one should I use for my production code?

For production code where you want to know if the entire batch succeeded, Result<Vec<T>, E> is appropriate because it propagates a single failure reason without needing manual error aggregation. If your business logic requires detailed reporting of which specific requests failed, then stick with Vec<Result<T>> and handle each error individually in your application layer instead of trying to force them into a single Result.
I'm trying to collect a vector of results into a single result but I don't know how to handle the errors properly.

You can use the map_err function on each element and then collect the whole collection. If you want any error in the list to cause the entire operation to fail, use map_err with the first error found or combine them into a single error value using something like BTreeMap or a custom type.
But what if I have multiple errors? Should I just take the first one and ignore the rest?

It depends on your strategy. If you use fail-first, you stop at the first error which is faster but might miss other issues. For fail-last, you collect all errors into a single container like a String or a custom Error type so the caller can see everything that went wrong.
I need to see an example of how to combine them using map_err and collect.

Here is the pattern: let results = vec![Err(A), Err(B), Ok(C)]; let combined = results.into_iter().map_err(|e| format!("{}", e)).collect::<Result<Vec<_>, _>(); This maps each error into a string, then collects it into a Result where success returns the vector and failure returns an error containing all messages.
Wait, I think I misunderstood map_err. It seems like I can't just use collect directly on errors because they are not compatible with Result.

You are correct that you cannot simply collect a vector of errors into a Result without mapping them first. The map_err function converts each Err to your desired error type before the collection happens, ensuring every element in the vector is either Ok or mapped to your target error type.
Okay so I need to use map_err on the iterator and then collect into a Result? But what if I want to keep all errors as separate items instead of combining them?

If you want to preserve individual errors, you should avoid mapping them into a single error type. Instead, you can collect the results directly into a Vec<Result<T>> which keeps each error distinct for later processing or logging.
This is getting frustrating I feel like I am missing something fundamental about how Rust handles these collections.

The key insight is that Rust does not automatically combine errors across different types. You must explicitly decide whether to propagate a single failure signal or accumulate multiple failures into a container type. Think of map_err as the bridge that transforms your element error into a compatible format for the final Result.
Got it so I should use map_err only when I need fail-first behavior and just collect normally if I want to keep all errors separate?
Exactly. Use map_err when you want one failure to stop execution immediately, otherwise let your vector hold the Vec<Result<T>> so each element retains its individual error state.
The CI/CD pipeline failed with this error message: 'error: `my_operation` returned Err, but you expected Ok. I need to fix this so the build passes.'

It looks like your code is returning a single Result<T> when it should be collecting multiple results into a vector of Results before converting them. Rust requires you to explicitly handle the conversion from a Vec<Result<T>> to a Result<Vec<T>> using the collect() method on an iterator or by manually mapping and collecting. The key difference between fail-first and fail-last is that fail-first returns immediately upon encountering any error, whereas fail-last collects all errors into the vector before returning one of them if there was at least one failure.
Okay, so I have a loop running `my_operation` multiple times and storing each result in a Vec. How do I combine these without stopping at the first error?

You should avoid using fail-first logic here since you want to collect all errors regardless of how many failed operations occurred. Instead of returning early, use `.collect::<Result<Vec<_>, _>>()` on an iterator that yields your results, or map each result to a variant like `Err(ChainError::from(result))` before collecting them into the final vector. This ensures you gather every error encountered in the loop.
I tried mapping to Err but I still get a compilation error saying 'cannot convert from Vec<Err> to Result<Vec<T>>'. What am I missing?

You need to ensure your iterator produces `Result` items consistently. If you are manually collecting results into a vector, you must first map each item in the vector using `.map(|r| r)` before calling `.collect::<Result<Vec<_>, _>>()`. The Rust compiler cannot automatically infer that every element is already a Result<T> if they are stored as Err values or mixed types. You need to explicitly transform your collection into an iterator of Results first.
Wait, so I shouldn't just collect the errors directly? What about using `collect()` on the vector itself?

No, you cannot call `.collect()` directly on a Vec<Err> because that would produce an empty Result. You must create an iterator from your results first and then collect it into the final type. If you want fail-last behavior, map each result to either Ok or Err as appropriate, then use `Vec::into_iter().collect::<Result<Vec<T>, E>>()` where T is your success value and E is your error type. This allows Rust to handle the conversion correctly.
But what if I want fail-first instead? Can I still collect all errors into a vector?

If you choose fail-first, you should not collect all errors into a single Result<Vec<T>> because that implies fail-last semantics. For fail-first, your loop should return immediately upon encountering the first Err and ignore subsequent results entirely. If you genuinely need to report multiple failures later in the application flow, then use fail-last by collecting them all before returning.
Okay, I understand now. But wait, what about the `ChainError` variant? Is that necessary for collecting errors?

The ChainError variant is useful when your error type is an enum with multiple variants like `Result<(), E1 | E2>`. In that case, you can construct a custom error by chaining individual failures using `.ok_or_else()` or manual pattern matching to build a comprehensive error message. However, for simple cases where all errors are of the same type, you do not need ChainError and can just collect them directly into a Vec<E> before wrapping in Result.

So I should avoid using `ChainError` unless my error types are heterogeneous? That makes sense. One more thing though, why does Rust require this explicit conversion step at all? Why not just let the compiler handle it automatically like Python or JavaScript?

Rust requires explicit conversions because its type system is statically typed and enforces strict safety guarantees through ownership and borrowing rules. Unlike dynamic languages where runtime errors might mask logic flaws, Rust ensures that error propagation is predictable at compile time by forcing you to explicitly define how errors are handled and combined. This prevents subtle bugs where unhandled errors silently propagate or duplicate errors are lost in the conversion process.
That's really helpful context! So the real fix for my CI pipeline was understanding that I needed to map each result before collecting them into a Result<Vec<T>>?

Exactly. Your original code likely collected Err values directly without mapping, which caused the type mismatch. By adding `.map(|r| r)` or similar logic before calling `.collect::<Result<Vec<_>, _>>()`, you ensure every element in your collection is properly typed as a Result<T> before being converted into the final Result<Vec<T>> structure. This satisfies Rust's strict type requirements and allows your pipeline to compile successfully.
Phew, that clears it up! I can see now why my code was failing. I'll add the map step before collecting and re-run the build.

Great progress! Remember though, always check if you intended fail-first or fail-last behavior when designing error handling logic. If you need to report all errors regardless of count, go for fail-last with explicit collection. If failing early is acceptable, avoid collecting beyond the first failure. Good luck with your CI/CD pipeline!

Hey, I'm reviewing a colleague's module that uses Result for individual operations. They're just collecting all the results into a Vec and returning it at the end, assuming if there are any errors in the vector then the whole thing fails. Is this actually bad practice?

That is indeed a common mistake for beginners who haven't looked deeply into error propagation traits yet. You shouldn't just return a Vec<Result<T>> from your function because that loses the specific information about which operation failed and how many errors occurred. Instead, you should use the combine_errors method on an iterator to transform a collection of individual results into a single Result<Vec<T>>. This way, if any error exists in the input vector, it is captured as one failure condition rather than being buried inside the data structure.
Okay so I should use collect with combine_errors? But what about when we want to stop early instead of waiting for all operations? Like if one fails, do we still try the rest?

You are absolutely right to distinguish between those two scenarios. If you need fail-first behavior where the function stops immediately upon encountering an error and returns that specific result, you should not use collect with combine_errors at all. In that case, you would manually iterate through your generator or iterator, checking the result of each operation before calling next(). As soon as a Result::Err is encountered, you can return it directly without trying further items. Only when you want to report every failure together do you use combine_errors on the collected vector.
Wait so does that mean I have to write a loop manually if I don't want to wait for all operations? Is there any way to make this more idiomatic without explicit loops?

Yes, there is a very idiomatic pattern using the iterator's next() method combined with the unwrap_or_else approach. You can chain multiple Result operations together using the ? operator inside your loop or use a helper function that takes an iterator and applies combine_errors only after confirming no errors were found during iteration. The key insight is that the ? operator automatically handles early termination by returning the first error it encounters, which makes your code much cleaner than writing manual loops with explicit break statements.
That sounds really helpful but I'm still confused about when to use unwrap_or_else versus just unwrapping in these cases. Can you clarify the difference?

The distinction between unwrap_or_else and plain unwrap depends entirely on your error handling strategy and whether you want to panic or recover gracefully. Unwrap will panic if any error exists, which is appropriate for internal logic where failure should not occur under normal conditions. However, unwrap_or_else allows you to provide a recovery function that transforms an Err into something else, like Ok(something) or logging the error before continuing. This flexibility makes it safer when dealing with external APIs or operations that might legitimately fail.
